title.emphasis: Part III: The Resource Logic of Collective Investment

section.para: The economic engine of the Investor Cooperative can be formally and precisely modeled using the tools of resource-sensitive logic. This provides a rigorous, auditable grammar for the flow of capital, ensuring that the cooperative's operations adhere to its constitutional principles of fairness and proportionality.

listitem.para: Investment as a Transformation: The act of investment is a linear implication (⊸) that consumes the capital resource and transforms it into a new set of resources: the asset itself and the associated risk. $C_{10k} ⊸ (PortfolioShare_A ⊗ Risk_A)

Returns as a Production Process: The financial return is another transformation that consumes the asset and produces the original principal plus a new surplus resource. PortfolioShare_A ⊸ ($C_{10k} ⊗ Surplus_A)

Distribution as a Just Transformation: The distribution of the surplus is the final step. The cooperative's democratic process defines the rule for this transformation. A simple rule might be: Surplus_A ⊸ (ManagementFee ⊗ NetReturn_A)

The Power of Relevance Logic: The use of relevance logic is critical here. It provides a formal guarantee that the NetReturn_A paid to a member is relevantly and causally derived from their initial contribution ($C_{10k}) and its subsequent transformations. It makes it logically impossible to justify distributing NetReturn_A to someone who did not make the initial contribution, thus providing a formal, provable basis for a desert-based system of justice.

section.para: This logical framework makes the cooperative's financial operations transparent and auditable in a way that goes beyond traditional accounting. It allows members to verify that the economic "grammar" of their constitution is being followed correctly.
